1: Psalm 46 is among the most influential psalms in Christian history: Martin Luther composed Ein feste Burg ist unser Gott (A Mighty Fortress Is Our God, 1529) based on it. The psalm's opening 'God is our refuge and strength, a help always present in trouble' became Luther's theological response to the Reformation's crisis. The 'Sons of Korah' superscription places it in the levitical singer tradition. The psalm describes cosmic dissolution and political chaos (vv. 2-3, 6) and responds with the Zion theology of YHWH's permanent presence in the city (vv. 4-5). 'Our refuge (machase) and strength (oz)' — two complementary images of YHWH's protective power: shelter and force.
2: 'Therefore we will not fear, even if the earth gives way, even if the mountains topple into the heart of the sea' — the cosmic dissolution scenario: the reversal of creation order. The earth 'giving way' (bemut arets, when the earth changes/is removed) and mountains falling into the sea are the images of the uncreation of the world — what happens when the chaotic waters overcome the boundaries established at creation. Ancient Near Eastern creation myths described the primordial chaos as the waters (Babylonian Tiamat) overcome by the divine warrior. Ps 46 uses this imagery to say: even if the entire creation unravels, YHWH's people will not fear. The spatial imagery of the cosmos itself collapsing is the maximally extreme case.
3: 'Though the waters roar and churn, though the mountains quake at the surging sea. Selah' — the continuation of the cosmic chaos image: roaring waters (yehemu meimav), mountains 'trembling' (yirashun harim) at the sea's pride/arrogance (bigavato). The Selah musical/liturgical pause here follows the maximally threatening image — the congregation pauses to take in the picture of cosmic dissolution before hearing the counter-image of the divine city's security (v. 4). The dramatic function of Selah as rhetorical pause is clear in this psalm's structure.
4: 'There is a river whose streams bring joy to the city of God, the sacred dwelling of the Most High' — the contrast: amid cosmic dissolution (vv. 2-3), a river (nahar) flows peacefully through the city of God. This river is not geographical (Jerusalem has no river; the nearest body of water is the Gihon spring and the Kidron valley). It is the river of Eden (Gen 2:10: the river watering the garden from which four branches flow) and anticipates the eschatological river of Rev 22:1-2 ('the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb'). The river signals that the city of God is the new Eden.
5: 'God is within her; she will not be shaken; God will help her when morning breaks' — 'God is within her' (Elohim bekerebah, God is in her midst) is the Zion theology's central claim: the divine presence (kavod) dwelling in the temple makes the city itself uniquely stable. The formula echoes Moses's prayer in Exod 33:15-16: 'If your Presence does not go with us, do not send us up from here.' Without the divine presence, the city is just a city; with it, it cannot be shaken. 'When morning breaks' (liphnot boqer) — the morning was the expected time of divine action in battle and the standard time of divine help in the Psalter (Ps 30:5; 59:16; 90:14).
6: 'Nations raged and kingdoms shook; he lifted his voice, and the earth dissolved' — the political chaos parallel to the cosmic chaos of vv. 2-3: raging nations and shaking kingdoms are the human-historical version of the dissolving earth and plunging mountains. But where the cosmic dissolution requires the river of v. 4 to calm, the political dissolution is resolved by a single act: YHWH lifting his voice (natan beqolo, gives his voice = speaks/thunders). The divine word of power dissolves the kingdoms that raged — the cosmic rebuke of Ps 18:15 and Isa 66:6. The contrast between the nations' rage and YHWH's single utterance establishes the complete disproportion between created power and divine authority.
7: 'The LORD of Armies (Yhwh Tseva'ot) is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. Selah' — the first of two identical refrains (also v. 11; a third refrain may have been present before v. 1 in some reconstructions). Yhwh Tseva'ot (LORD of Hosts/Armies) — the divine commander of heavenly and earthly armies (cf. Ps 24:10). 'The God of Jacob' (Elohei ya'aqov) — the covenant God of the patriarchs who chose the specific, particular people of Israel. The two divine titles work together: cosmic commander + covenant patriarchal God. 'Fortress' (misgav, high-place/stronghold) is the third protection image in the psalm after 'refuge' and 'strength' (v. 1).
8: 'Come and see what the LORD has done, what desolations he has brought upon the earth' — the invitation to observe YHWH's acts in history. 'Desolations' (shammot, wastes/ruins) that YHWH has caused are not random destruction but the ruins left by his defeat of the raging nations (v. 6). The command 'come and see' (lechu chazu) is the prophetic call to empirical observation of YHWH's historical interventions: the ruins are evidence that the divine warrior has been active. Ps 66:5 uses the same call: 'Come and see what God has done.'
9: 'He puts an end to wars across the whole earth; he breaks bows and cuts spears apart; he burns the war chariots' — the eschatological disarmament: YHWH ending wars and destroying weapons throughout the earth. The broken bow, cut spear, and burned chariot are the three principal weapons of ancient Near Eastern warfare — covering long-range (bow), close-range (spear), and mobile (chariot) military capacity. This verse is the Psalmic parallel to Isa 2:4 ('They will beat their swords into plowshares') and Mic 4:3 — the prophetic vision of universal peace through divine disarmament. The weapons are not laid down voluntarily but destroyed by YHWH.
10: 'Be still, and know that I am God; I will be exalted among the nations; I will be exalted throughout the earth' — 'Be still' (harpu) is not primarily a devotional call to quiet prayer but a command in the military context: 'stop fighting, stand down, cease resistance.' The full meaning: 'Let your hands drop (stop fighting) and know that I am God.' YHWH's word to the raging nations is the command to acknowledge his sovereignty. 'I will be exalted among the nations' (arum bagoyim) — the eschatological outcome of all history: universal recognition of divine sovereignty. This verse functions as YHWH's own speech within the Psalm.
11: 'The LORD of Armies is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. Selah' — the closing refrain (identical to v. 7) brackets the central content (vv. 8-10: YHWH's historical desolations and eschatological disarmament) with the covenant community's confession. The double declaration — YHWH with us, YHWH our fortress — is the theological anchor against which both cosmic dissolution (vv. 2-3) and political chaos (v. 6) are measured and found wanting. The final Selah closes the psalm with space for the confession to resonate.
